entry.rationale: Mary Reese died 22 June 1933. New York State death certificates from this period routinely record the names of both parents, including the mother's maiden name, making this the single most direct source for identifying her parents. FamilySearch holds the New York State Death Index 1880-1956 (collection 2803479), fully indexed and searchable.

The 1880 US Federal Census was taken in June 1880. If Mary was born circa 1880 she may appear as an infant (age 0-1) in her parents' household, directly identifying them by name, relationship, and birthplace. FamilySearch has this census fully indexed.

New York conducted a state census in 1892. Mary would be approximately 12 years old and very likely still living in her parents' household, identifying both parents and any siblings then at home. FamilySearch collection 1529100 covers 1892-1915 NY state censuses.

The 1900 US Federal Census recorded each person's birth month and year, and listed every household member's relationship to the head, birthplace, and parents' birthplaces. Mary would be approximately 20 years old — likely still at home or nearby — providing confirmation of her parents' identities and corroborating the 1880 and 1892 census findings.

New York began statewide civil birth registration in 1880, though early compliance was uneven. A birth record for Mary Reese circa 1880 would name both parents directly and constitute original evidence of parentage. FamilySearch collection 4460198 (Birth Indexes outside NYC, 1881-1942) and collection 2240282 (NYC Births, 1846-1909) cover this period depending on whether she was born inside or outside New York City.

If Mary Reese married, her New York marriage certificate would record her parents' names and her father's birthplace. FamilySearch collection 1618491 (NY County Marriages, 1908-1937) covers most of the plausible marriage window. Finding a marriage record would corroborate the death certificate's parental information and potentially open new leads for her siblings.

Per standard parentage research practice, a dedicated search for the parents' marriage record is required. Their marriage certificate would (a) confirm the couple as a unit, (b) supply the mother's maiden name (which census and death records often omit), and (c) by its date relative to Mary's 1880 birth, corroborate a father otherwise named only indirectly. FamilySearch collection 1618491 (County Marriages) and 2373836 (Church and Civil Marriages) cover this window. This item depends on identifying the parents' names first (pli_001 through pli_005).

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Parents of Mary "May" Reese (born c.1880 New York; died 22 June 1933)

**Conclusion (probable):** Mary Reese was the daughter of **Lewis Joseph Reese** (also known as Louis Reese; born circa 1847, New York; FamilySearch person LVF8-NQJ) and **Laura Amelia Scott** (born circa 1850, New York; FamilySearch person L6JB-YHG).

### Evidence

**Source 1 — 1880 US Federal Census** (derivative index; ark:/61903/1:1:MZ6Q-1XV).  
The 1880 New York City census enumerates the household of Louis Reese (head, shoemaker, born 1847 New York) and Laura Reese (wife, born 1850 New York), with four children including **May** (born ~1880) [assertions a_035, a_036]. The relationship column records May as "child" of the head. "May" is a standard 19th-century nickname for Mary. *Source class: derivative index of an original census; information indeterminate (household informant unknown); evidence direct (explicit relationship column).*

**Source 2 — 1898 NYC Marriage Record, Mary/May Reese** (derivative index; ark:/61903/1:1:Q2CX-S23P; image ark:/61903/1:2:QL74-B9ZL).  
The marriage record of Joseph Whelan and May Reese, 14 October 1898, Queens County, names the bride's father as **Lewis Reese** and the bride's mother as **Laura Amelia Scott** [a_065, a_066, a_068, a_069]. The bride is the person best placed to know her parents' names. An indexed birth year of 1897 is an indexing error — the 1880 census places May Reese in this household as an infant; 1877 is the likely correct reading. The birth-year anomaly does not affect the parentage entry. *Source class: derivative index; information primary (bride's own statement); evidence direct.*

**Source 3 — 1899 NYC Marriage Record, Lewis Reese Jr.** (derivative index; ark:/61903/1:1:Q2CV-64HP).  
The marriage record of Lewis Reese and Susan A. Smith, 26 March 1899, Kings County, names the groom's father as **Lewis Reese** and the groom's mother as **Laura Scott** [a_110, a_111, a_114, a_115]. The groom was born circa 1878 New York — matching the child Louis in the same 1880 household. An independent informant on a separate occasion names the same father and the same mother's maiden name. *Source class: derivative index; information primary (groom's own statement); evidence direct.*

**Source 4 — 1900 NYC Marriage Record, Elizabeth J. Reese** (derivative index; ark:/61903/1:1:Q2CV-XFWG).  
The marriage record of William K. Masten and Elizabeth J. Reese, [1900], Kings County, names the bride's father as **Lewis Reese** and the bride's mother as **Laura Amelia Scott** [a_078, a_079, a_082, a_083]. Elizabeth was born circa 1876 New York — matching the child Lizzie in the 1880 household. A second sibling, on a separate occasion, names the same two parents with the same maternal maiden name. *Source class: derivative index; information primary (bride's own statement); evidence direct.*

**Source 5 — 1906 NYC Marriage Record, Fannie Reese** (derivative index; ark:/61903/1:1:Q2CV-TDKT).  
The marriage record of William Peter Friedman and Fannie Reese, 22 February 1906, Kings County, names the bride's father as **Louis** [Reese] and the bride's mother as **Laura Scott** [a_094, a_095, a_096, a_098]. A third sibling, on yet another separate occasion, names the same parents. *Source class: derivative index; information primary (bride's own statement); evidence direct.*

### Correlation

Five independent sources spanning 26 years (1880–1906) agree that the parents of Mary/May Reese were Lewis/Louis Reese and Laura (Scott) Reese. The sources are independent: the census was created by an enumerator observing the household; the four marriage records were created on separate occasions by four different siblings each acting as informant for their own marriage. None of the siblings' records could have been derived from another.

The father's given name varies between "Lewis" and "Louis" — a documented interchangeable pair in 19th-century New York records, not a conflict. Three of the four marriage records record the mother's maiden name as "Scott"; the 1906 Friedman record gives simply "Laura Scott," consistent. The mother's full given name "Laura Amelia" appears in two records (1898 and 1900); the 1899 and 1906 records record only "Laura," which is not contradictory.

The 1880 census establishes co-residence and the direct "child" relationship column; it does not independently name the mother as a parent of May (that link is inferred from Laura's role as wife of the head). The four marriage records supply direct, primary parentage statements.

### Limitations

All sources are FamilySearch derivative index entries. No original record images have been examined; transcription errors cannot be independently verified. The original 1898 marriage register image (ark:/61903/1:2:QL74-B9ZL) is digitized on FamilySearch and should be consulted to confirm the indexed parental names — examining it would likely advance this conclusion to **proved**. Church and probate records were not searched. The parents' own marriage was not located (likely predates indexed coverage).

### Conclusion

**Probable.** The preponderance of evidence from four independent primary-information sources, corroborated by the 1880 household census, establishes that Mary "May" Reese (born circa 1880, New York; died 22 June 1933) was the daughter of Lewis Joseph Reese (LVF8-NQJ) and Laura Amelia Scott (L6JB-YHG). The tier is probable rather than proved because all sources are derivative indexes; GPS Component 4 (original sources) is not yet fully met. *To advance to proved: examine the original 1898 NYC marriage register image (ark:/61903/1:2:QL74-B9ZL).*

entry.question: Who were all the children of Lewis Joseph Reese (born circa 1847, New York, died 1912) and Laura Amelia Scott (born circa 1850, New York)?
entry.rationale: q_001 established at probable tier that Mary Reese's parents were Lewis Joseph Reese (LVF8-NQJ) and Laura Amelia Scott (L6JB-YHG). The project objective requires identifying all of Mary's siblings, which are the other children of this couple. Research to date has incidentally identified six likely siblings: Clarence (b.~1869), Elizabeth/Lizzie (b.~1876), Lewis Jr. (b.~1878), an unnamed child in the 1892 census (b.~1883), and Fannie (b.~1887). A systematic enumeration requires searching census records for the Lewis Reese household across all available census years (1880, 1892, 1900, 1905, 1910), plus NYC birth indexes (1880-1895) and death records for any deceased children. FamilySearch indexes these collections. The 1910 census following the father's 1912 death and any surviving adult children's later records will complete the picture.

New York conducted a state census in June 1905. Lewis Joseph Reese (LVF8-NQJ, b.~1847) and Laura Amelia Scott (L6JB-YHG, b.~1850) were both alive and likely still in Brooklyn. This census would list all household members including any children still at home between the 1900 federal census and the father's 1912 death — the critical window for identifying late-born children (any born 1887–1905) and confirming which earlier children remained. FamilySearch collection 1529100 (New York, State Census, 1892–1915) covers this year.

The 1910 US census, taken two years before Lewis Sr.'s October 1912 death, is likely the last census capturing his household intact. It would show any children still at home, confirm the family's Brooklyn address, and provide birth years for all members. Adult children who had already left would appear in their own households — searching for Clarence (b.~1869), Elizabeth (b.~1876), Lewis Jr. (b.~1878), and Fannie (b.~1887) in their own 1910 households would confirm them as siblings and establish their families. FamilySearch collection 1727033 (United States Census, 1910).

Lewis Joseph Reese died 27 October 1912 in Brooklyn (known from tree data). His death certificate or NYC Municipal Death record would name the informant (often a child) and may list survivors. The death register entry for a family patriarch frequently identifies adult children still in contact with the family. FamilySearch collection 2240477 (New York, New York City Municipal Deaths, 1795–1949) covers Brooklyn 1912.

The 1920 census, taken eight years after Lewis Sr.'s death, would show surviving adult siblings in their own households. Searching for Clarence Reese (b.~1869, not seen after 1880), Elizabeth Masten (née Reese, b.~1876), Lewis Reese Jr. (b.~1878), and Fannie Friedman (née Reese, b.~1887) in 1920 would confirm their survival and family composition. Any previously unidentified siblings might also appear. FamilySearch collection 1488411 (United States Census, 1920).

NYC birth records may reveal additional children of Lewis and Laura Reese born after the June 1880 census and before 1900. The 1892 census shows a child (I4) born ~1883, and Fannie Reese born ~1887 was found only via her 1906 marriage record. A targeted search for births with father 'Reese' or 'Reise' in NYC 1880–1900 could surface additional children not captured in household census snapshots, including any who died in childhood before appearing in a census. FamilySearch collection 2240282 (New York, New York City Births, 1846–1909) and collection 4460198 (New York, Birth Index, 1881–1942).

Clarence Reese (b.~1869), the eldest child identified in the 1880 census, has not appeared in any subsequent record. As the oldest he would have left the parental home earliest. A targeted search for Clarence Reese in the 1900, 1910, and 1920 census records — and in NYC marriage records — would confirm he is a sibling, identify his fate (alive, deceased, emigrated), and potentially surface additional Reese family associates. His absence from post-1880 records is the largest gap in the current sibling list.

NYC death records may identify children of Lewis and Laura Reese who died in childhood or early adulthood and therefore do not appear in adult census records. A search in NYC Municipal Deaths (collection 2240477) for 'Reese' children with ages 0–20 and father named Lewis/Louis Reese would surface any such cases, completing the picture of the full sibling set. This also covers death records for Laura Amelia Scott Reese (death date unknown), whose death certificate might list surviving children.
